article_title: Max Planck Team Maps Cell-Surface Sugar Code at Nanometre Resolution, Revealing Cancer and Immune Signatures
summary: Submission approvable with 2 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

findings.message: Source redirected to different domain: nature.com

content_preview.title: Max Planck Team Maps Cell-Surface Sugar Code at Nanometre Resolution, Revealing Cancer and Immune Signatures
content_preview.summary: Scientists have developed Glycan Atlasing, a super-resolution technique that reads shifting sugar patterns on cell surfaces to distinguish cancer stages from healthy tissue and activated from resting immune cells.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Every human cell is wrapped in a thin sugar coating called the glycocalyx — a constantly shifting molecular layer that mediates interactions with its environment. Scientists at the Max Planck Institute for the Science of Light have now shown that the nanoscale arrangement of those sugars is not random: it encodes information about whether a cell is healthy, cancerous, or immunologically active. The team's new technique, called Glycan Atlasing, uses state-of-the-art super-resolution ...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News article with appropriate technical depth. The piece covers the study from multiple angles (technique, findings, limitations, future directions) and correctly scopes claims to what the research demonstrated. The Analysis section adds editorial context without sensationalising. Word count (706) fits the News category range (400-1200).
editor_notes.source_verification: source-0.html.gz (ScienceDaily): Confirmed. Supports: Möckl as group head, Glycan Atlasing technique, cell culture/blood cell/tissue sample scope, 'display screen' metaphor quote, Möckl's future-studies quote. source-1 (phys.org): Snapshot failed (HTTP 403 bot-block); no archive fallback available. Claims attributed to phys.org (three-capability list, first-technique-to-demonstrate spatial arrangement) are substantially corroborated by source-0 (ScienceDaily) and source-4 (Nature paper abstract). source-2.html.gz (NanotechnologyWorld): Confirmed. Supports: DNA-barcoded lectin labelling + metabolic oligosaccharide engineering method, ~9 Å ångström-scale resolution, Yurekli-Hashem quote verbatim. NOTE: NanotechnologyWorld itself misidentifies Yurekli and Hashem as 'first authors' — the article inherited this error. The Nature paper (source-4) clearly states that Dijo Moonnukandathil Joseph and Nazlican Yurekli contributed equally as co-first authors; Reem Hashem is a regular co-author. source-3.html.gz (NewKerala): Confirmed. Supports: Möckl future-studies quote verbatim, overview of findings. source-4.html.gz (Nature Nanotechnology via doi.org → nature.com): Confirmed. Paper title 'Glycan atlassing enables functional tracing of cell state', published 14 May 2026, Nature Nanotechnology vol. 21 pp. 720-731. Authors: Dijo Moonnukandathil Joseph and Nazlican Yurekli are listed as co-first authors (contributed equally); Reem Hashem is third author without equal-contribution notation. Confirms DNA-PAINT microscopy, lectin labelling + metabolic oligosaccharide engineering, sialic acid targeting, glycocalyx abstract claims.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All central claims verified against snapshots. One factual error identified: the article states 'First authors Yurekli and Hashem described the significance...' (attributing the quote to 'first authors'). The Nature paper (primary source) establishes that Joseph and Yurekli are the co-first authors (equal contribution); Hashem is a co-author but not a first author. The NanotechnologyWorld source also incorrectly calls them 'first authors' — the article copied this secondary-source error. The quote itself is verbatim and correctly attributed to Yurekli and Hashem; only the 'first authors' characterisation is wrong. The technique spelling 'Glycan Atlasing' (single-s) appears in secondary sources; the Nature paper uses 'Glycan atlassing' (double-s). This is an acceptable secondary-source variant and not a corrections-level issue. No fabricated quotes or hallucinated statistics detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference, appropriate hedging on diagnostic potential ('suggests a potential path', 'if those differences prove diagnostically stable').
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate or substantially overlapping coverage found in the archive. A previous Max Planck article (May 5, ICARUS 2.0 satellite) covers a different institute and topic. The glycan/glycocalyx subject is new to the archive.
concerns: The article misidentifies the first authors: it says 'First authors Yurekli and Hashem' but the Nature paper (primary source) confirms that Dijo Moonnukandathil Joseph and Nazlican Yurekli are the co-first authors (equal contribution), while Reem Hashem is a regular co-author. This error was inherited from the NanotechnologyWorld secondary source and is not supported by the primary source.

Two sources (nanotechnologyworld.org and newkerala.com) are not in the source allowlist. Both are legitimate secondary aggregators of the same Max Planck press release; their content is corroborated by ScienceDaily and the Nature paper. The doi.org source correctly redirects to nature.com, which is allowlisted.
Phys.org source (source-1) returned HTTP 403 and no snapshot is available. Claims attributed to phys.org are corroborated by other captured sources.
recommendations: File a corrections record noting the first-author misidentification: Joseph and Yurekli are co-first authors; Hashem is a co-author.
Consider adding nanotechnologyworld.org and newkerala.com to config/source_allowlist.txt after confirming editorial standards.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Substantively sound article covering a genuine research advance published in Nature Nanotechnology. The central claim, method description, and findings are all well-sourced and accurately reported. The sole factual error — misidentifying Reem Hashem as a first author when she is a co-author — is a recoverable subordinate-body error inherited from a secondary source. The primary source (Nature paper) is captured and confirms the correct authorship. Verdict: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS with a corrections record documenting the first-author misidentification.
